title: A2A (Agent-to-Agent Protocol)
short_definition: Google's open protocol for letting AI agents from different vendors discover each other and collaborate on tasks — the agent-to-agent equivalent of MCP.
full_explanation:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) standardized how a single agent talks to tools. A2A, announced by Google in early 2025, addresses the next problem: how do agents from different vendors talk to each other? Without a standard, every multi-agent integration is bespoke — your CRM agent doesn't know how to call your billing agent if they were built by different teams.

A2A defines a discovery mechanism (Agent Cards — JSON-LD documents at well-known URLs describing each agent's capabilities), a task-delegation protocol (one agent submits a task to another, gets back results or status), and an authentication model (OAuth + agent identities). The protocol is HTTP-based and intentionally simple to implement.

Launched with backers including Atlassian, Box, Cohere, JetBrains, MongoDB, PayPal, Salesforce, SAP, ServiceNow, and Workday — the major SaaS vendors building agent-style integrations. Adoption is early; most production multi-agent systems still use bespoke wiring. But A2A is the strongest standardization push so far for cross-vendor agent collaboration.
why_it_matters: If multi-agent systems are going to scale beyond single-vendor walled gardens, a standard is needed. A2A is the strongest contender. For practitioners building agents that need to interoperate with other vendors' agents — or building agents intended to be discoverable by other systems — implementing A2A early positions your system to plug into the emerging ecosystem. Less relevant for single-vendor agent stacks; very relevant for SaaS platforms wanting to expose agent capabilities to customer agents.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of A2A as DNS + HTTP for agents. DNS lets you discover servers by name; HTTP lets browsers talk to them. A2A's Agent Cards are like DNS for agents (find them by capability), and A2A's task protocol is like HTTP for them (call them with structured requests). Without these, every agent integration is hand-wired; with them, agents can discover and call each other across organizational boundaries.
see_it_in_practice: A2A spec lives at github.com/google/a2a. Reference implementations exist in Python, TypeScript, and Java. An Agent Card is a JSON-LD doc at /.well-known/agent.json describing what the agent does and how to invoke it.

related_terms_explained.connection: MCP standardizes single-agent-to-tools; A2A standardizes agent-to-agent.
Agent Card is the discovery document format defined by A2A.
A2A is the protocol layer enabling cross-vendor agent collaboration.
A2A treats other agents like tools but with richer task semantics.

title: Admin API (OpenAI / Anthropic)
short_definition: API endpoints for managing your account programmatically — creating API keys, monitoring usage, managing team members, configuring rate limits.
full_explanation:
Distinct from inference APIs (which run prompts), Admin APIs let you manage your account without using the dashboard. OpenAI's Admin API covers users, projects, API keys, audit logs, and usage data. Anthropic's Workspaces API similarly handles workspace creation, member management, and key rotation. Both require an admin-level key (separate from inference keys).

Use cases: provisioning per-customer API keys for a SaaS app, automated quota monitoring, programmatic key rotation for security policy, exporting usage data for cost allocation across teams. Companies running multi-tenant LLM services use these heavily; individual developers rarely touch them.

Production architectures often combine: Admin API to provision a per-customer API key with strict rate limits, then route customer traffic through that key. Usage tracked per-key gives accurate per-customer cost attribution. Key rotation via the Admin API reduces blast radius if any single key leaks.
why_it_matters: If you're building multi-tenant LLM products or shipping LLM features at organizational scale, Admin APIs are how you build proper account hygiene without manual dashboard work. Anyone managing more than a few API keys benefits from automating key lifecycle through these APIs. Less relevant for individual developers; essential for teams running platforms.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of inference APIs as the cash register and Admin APIs as the back-office software that creates registers, hires cashiers, and reads daily receipts. The cash register is what customers interact with; the back office runs the business. For one-person shops, you don't need much back-office tooling. For chains, you can't operate without it.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI Admin: `curl https://api.openai.com/v1/organization/api_keys -H 'Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_ADMIN_KEY'`. Lists all keys in the org with usage data. Anthropic Workspaces: `curl https://api.anthropic.com/v1/organizations/workspaces -H 'x-api-key: $ANTHROPIC_ADMIN_KEY' -H 'anthropic-version: 2023-06-01'`.

related_terms_explained.connection: Admin APIs let you configure per-key rate limits programmatically.
Admin APIs are the meta-layer for managing other APIs.

title: Agent Card
short_definition: A standardized JSON-LD document describing an AI agent's capabilities, endpoints, and authentication — the discovery layer of Google's A2A protocol.
full_explanation:
A2A protocol's discovery mechanism. Each agent publishes an Agent Card at a well-known URL (`/.well-known/agent.json`) describing: what tasks it can perform, what input format it expects, what output format it returns, what authentication it requires, what protocol versions it supports.

The schema is standardized JSON-LD, allowing other agents to parse and reason about capabilities programmatically. An Agent Card might list capabilities as 'translate-document', 'summarize-meeting', 'schedule-event' — each with an input schema, output schema, and any required permissions.

The goal: enable an agent to discover and call other agents without manual integration. Your travel-planning agent looks up the cards for restaurant-booking, hotel-booking, and flight-booking agents; sees what they accept; calls them in sequence. No bespoke API client per agent; just A2A protocol speakers reading cards.

Adoption is early — A2A launched 2025 — but the pattern parallels OpenAPI (HTTP API discovery) and DNS records (server discovery). For agents to genuinely interoperate at scale, something like Agent Cards is necessary infrastructure.
why_it_matters: If you're building agent infrastructure intended to be called by other agents — a SaaS exposing agent capabilities to customer-built agents — publishing an Agent Card positions you in the emerging A2A ecosystem. For agents intended to call other agents, parsing Agent Cards lets you skip per-vendor integration code. Early but worth tracking; the equivalent moment to OpenAPI's emergence in 2014.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of an Agent Card like a business card with a machine-readable QR code on the back. Humans read 'Joe — Translation Specialist'; machines scan the QR and get the full address, phone, available hours, and price list. Agent Cards are the QR code for agents — discovery and capability declaration in one document.
see_it_in_practice: Spec at github.com/google/a2a. To publish: serve a JSON-LD doc at `/.well-known/agent.json` with capability descriptions. To consume: GET that URL on any A2A-compatible agent and parse the response.

related_terms_explained.connection: Agent Cards are the discovery document defined by the A2A protocol.
Agent Cards describe agent capabilities to other agents.
Similar idea — OpenAPI describes HTTP APIs, Agent Card describes agent capabilities.

title: Anthropic API Prompt Caching
short_definition: Anthropic's feature that caches a prompt prefix on their servers and charges 90% less for cache hits — saves cost and latency on repeated context.
full_explanation:
Anthropic's prompt caching (launched 2024) lets you mark portions of your prompt as cacheable. The first request with a particular cached prefix costs slightly more (cache write); subsequent requests within 5 minutes (configurable to 1h) that include the same prefix cost 90% less for the cached portion. Cache lifetime resets on each hit.

Use cases: long system prompts shared across many user queries (RAG with the same documents repeatedly, agent loops with fixed instructions, batch evaluation runs). Mark the static part with `cache_control: {type: 'ephemeral'}` in the message structure; the API handles the rest.

Cost math: on Claude Sonnet 4 ($3/$15 per 1M input/output), cached input is $0.30/1M (90% off). For a RAG application with a 10K-token retrieved context that gets reused across 20 follow-up questions, prompt caching saves ~80-90% on input cost. The cache write surcharge (25%) pays back after just 1-2 cache hits.

OpenAI launched a similar feature in late 2024 (automatic, no cache_control marker). Both providers are converging on prompt caching as standard infrastructure for repeat-context workloads.
why_it_matters: For any application with repeated long prompts — RAG with stable context windows, agent loops, evaluation harnesses, multi-turn chat with stable system prompts — prompt caching is the single biggest cost optimization available. 30-second integration; 50-90% cost savings on the right workloads. For practitioners running production Claude apps, knowing exactly where to mark cache breakpoints is mandatory infrastructure literacy.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of prompt caching like prep work in a restaurant kitchen. The chef pre-chops the same vegetables for the daily soup once in the morning. Each customer order gets fresh assembly but reuses the prep. If you order soup, you save the chopping time. Without prep, every order starts from raw ingredients. Caching is the same principle: pre-process the static parts, charge less for the assembly per order.
see_it_in_practice: Anthropic Python SDK: `messages=[{'role':'user','content':[{'type':'text','text':'long static context...','cache_control':{'type':'ephemeral'}}, {'type':'text','text':'specific question'}]}]`. The first big text block gets cached; per-question text doesn't. Subsequent requests reusing the same big text hit cache.

related_terms_explained.connection: Anthropic prompt caching is one specific implementation; OpenAI has theirs.
Cache control is set within Anthropic's Messages API request structure.
Prompt caching is the highest-impact LLM cost-reduction feature for repeated-context apps.
RAG with stable retrieved context is one of the best use cases for prompt caching.

title: API
short_definition: An Application Programming Interface — the URL endpoint and request format you use to call a service like an LLM provider's model from your own code.
full_explanation:
An API is a contract: send this request to this URL, receive this response. For LLMs, the typical contract is a POST request to a chat-completions endpoint with JSON body containing model name, messages, and parameters; the response is JSON with the generated text and metadata.

The major LLM API surfaces:
- **OpenAI Chat Completions**: the de facto standard format adopted by many open-source projects (vLLM, Ollama, etc.) for compatibility.
- **Anthropic Messages API**: similar but with more nuanced multimodal content blocks.
- **Google Gemini API**: similar request structure with Google-specific extensions.
- **Open-source compatible APIs**: vLLM, Ollama, llama.cpp's HTTP server, all provide OpenAI-compatible endpoints.

The practical impact is portability: code written against the OpenAI API often works against vLLM or Ollama with just a base URL change. This is why 'OpenAI compatible' became a near-universal feature of open-source serving — it lets users swap providers without rewriting application code.
why_it_matters: Foundational concept. Every application that uses an LLM does so via an API. Understanding the request/response shape, authentication, error handling, and rate limiting is the bare minimum literacy for any LLM developer. The OpenAI Chat Completions format specifically has become so dominant that knowing it means you can use most LLM providers and self-hosted models without learning new APIs.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of an API like a vending machine. Press button A6, get a Snickers. Press button B2, get a Coke. The machine has well-defined inputs (buttons) and outputs (snacks). LLM APIs are vending machines for text: send a prompt with parameters, get a generation. The buttons are URL paths; the snacks are model responses; the contract is the JSON schema. Different machines accept different button layouts (Anthropic vs OpenAI vs Google), but the principle is identical.
see_it_in_practice: Hello world: `curl https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions -H 'Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"model":"gpt-4o","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}]}'`. That's the entire API surface for chat completions.

related_terms_explained.connection: Chat Completions is OpenAI's primary API endpoint and the de facto standard.
Messages API is Anthropic's equivalent of Chat Completions.
All LLM APIs enforce rate limits to prevent overuse.
Streaming is an extension of standard API request/response for real-time output.

title: Assistants API (OpenAI)
short_definition: OpenAI's higher-level API that manages threads, tools, and file uploads server-side — abstracts conversation state so you don't have to.
full_explanation:
Standard Chat Completions API requires you to manage conversation history in your application code: every request, send the full message history. The Assistants API moves that state to OpenAI's servers. You create an Assistant (system instructions + tool config + model), open Threads (conversations), add Messages, and run completions. OpenAI manages the message history, file context, and tool execution under the hood.

Features Assistants offers beyond Chat Completions: persistent threads (no need to send history with each call), built-in code interpreter, built-in file search (RAG over uploaded docs), and function calling with state across runs. The downside is vendor lock-in (no equivalent on Anthropic, Google, or self-hosted models) and less control over context — you can't easily inject middleware or modify history.

In 2025 OpenAI announced the Responses API (a unified successor combining Chat Completions and Assistants features). Assistants API is in transition; new projects often start with Responses or fall back to Chat Completions for portability. For existing Assistants projects, migration is gradual.
why_it_matters: Understanding the Assistants vs Chat Completions split helps you reason about state management, vendor lock-in, and architecture choices. For new projects, Chat Completions is more portable; Assistants offers more built-in features at the cost of OpenAI dependency. For practitioners evaluating LLM platforms, this distinction is a meaningful architecture decision — pick based on whether you value portability or built-in convenience more.
how_to_think_about_it: Chat Completions is like sending a postcard with the full conversation history every time — stateless, you carry the state in your hand. Assistants is like keeping a notebook at OpenAI's office — you walk in, they remember your previous visits, you continue where you left off. Notebook is convenient but only works at this office. Postcards work at any post office (any LLM provider).
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: `assistant = client.beta.assistants.create(name='Math Tutor', instructions='Help with math', tools=[{'type':'code_interpreter'}], model='gpt-4o'); thread = client.beta.threads.create(); client.beta.threads.messages.create(thread_id=thread.id, role='user', content='Solve x^2=4'); run = client.beta.threads.runs.create(thread_id=thread.id, assistant_id=assistant.id)`.

related_terms_explained.connection: The lower-level alternative — Chat Completions is stateless, Assistants is stateful.
OpenAI's newer API that consolidates Chat Completions and Assistants features.
Assistants API has built-in code interpreter and file search tools.
Assistants API includes file_search as a built-in RAG feature.

title: Batch API
short_definition: An API mode where you submit many requests at once to be processed asynchronously — usually 50% cheaper than real-time inference, with up to 24h turnaround.
full_explanation:
Real-time LLM API calls are expensive partly because providers must reserve capacity for sub-second response. For non-urgent workloads (batch evaluation, bulk content generation, offline analysis), reservations are wasteful. Batch APIs let you submit thousands of requests as a single job, process them off-peak, and collect results within 24 hours. The discount: 50% off both input and output rates.

Workflow: create a JSONL file with one request per line, upload to the provider, get a batch job ID, poll for completion (typically minutes to hours), download results JSONL. OpenAI and Anthropic both support batch APIs. Anthropic's batch API additionally supports per-request prompt caching, multiplying cost savings.

Good fits: nightly evaluation runs, RAG document preprocessing, content moderation backfill, dataset generation for fine-tuning, A/B test scoring across thousands of prompts. Bad fits: anything user-facing or interactive.

Production pattern: route urgent traffic to real-time API, batch the rest. For data engineering teams running daily ETL jobs that touch LLMs, batch APIs cut costs in half with a few lines of code change.
why_it_matters: If you have any non-urgent LLM workloads, batch APIs cut the cost in half. For data pipelines, evaluation runs, content backfills, or scheduled processing, this is essentially free money — same model, same quality, half the price. Most teams don't think about this until their LLM bill grows uncomfortable; the right time is before that. Five-minute integration, 50% recurring savings.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of batch APIs like overnight shipping rates vs same-day. Same package gets to the same destination, but if you can wait, you pay 50% less. The provider can use cheap off-peak compute (overnight in their cluster). For data pipelines and bulk operations where 'right now' isn't required, batch APIs are the economically rational choice.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: write requests to JSONL, `client.files.create(file=open('batch.jsonl','rb'), purpose='batch'); batch = client.batches.create(input_file_id=file.id, endpoint='/v1/chat/completions', completion_window='24h')`. Poll batch.status until 'completed', download via `client.files.content(batch.output_file_id)`.

related_terms_explained.connection: OpenAI's specific batch API implementation.
Batch APIs offer the largest single discount on token cost (50%).
Batch APIs typically wrap Chat Completions endpoints with async batching.
Anthropic's batch API supports per-request prompt caching for compounding savings.

title: Chat Completions API
short_definition: OpenAI's primary API for sending messages to a model and receiving generated text — the de facto standard request format for the LLM industry.
full_explanation:
OpenAI's Chat Completions API (POST /v1/chat/completions) takes a list of messages with roles (system, user, assistant, tool) and returns one or more generated assistant messages. The request format is now ubiquitous: vLLM, Ollama, LM Studio, llama.cpp's server, and dozens of other open-source projects all expose 'OpenAI-compatible' endpoints that accept the same JSON.

Key request fields: `model`, `messages`, `temperature`, `top_p`, `max_tokens`, `tools` (function calling), `response_format` (JSON mode / structured output), `stream` (SSE streaming). Response includes generated content, finish reason (stop/length/tool_calls), and token usage stats.

Why 'chat' specifically: the first generation of LLM APIs (Completions, the predecessor) treated text as a flat stream. Chat Completions added structured roles (system/user/assistant) which match how chat-tuned models actually want to be prompted. The structured format also handles multi-turn conversation cleanly without manual delimiter management.

For anyone building LLM applications in 2024-2026, Chat Completions is essentially the lingua franca. Even Anthropic's distinct Messages API is structurally similar enough that translating between them is mechanical.
why_it_matters: The Chat Completions request format is one of the most-used JSON schemas in the AI industry. Knowing it lets you call OpenAI, vLLM, Ollama, LiteLLM, and dozens of other compatible endpoints with the same code. For practitioners, this is mandatory baseline literacy. Even if you're using Anthropic's Messages API or Google's Gemini API as your primary, knowing Chat Completions means you can swap to or from OpenAI-compatible endpoints with minimal friction.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of Chat Completions as the QWERTY keyboard of LLM APIs. Other layouts exist (Dvorak = Anthropic Messages), but most people have learned QWERTY because most keyboards use it. The format won not because it's the best possible design but because it's the most-supported. Compatibility wins.
see_it_in_practice: Three lines: `import openai; client = openai.Client(); resp = client.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-4o', messages=[{'role':'user','content':'Hello'}])`. Swap base_url to use vLLM, Ollama, or any compatible endpoint with the same code.

related_terms_explained.connection: Chat Completions is the most-used LLM API endpoint.
Anthropic's parallel API; structurally similar but with different field names.
Chat Completions handles tool/function calling via the tools field.
Chat Completions supports structured output via response_format.
Chat Completions supports SSE streaming via stream:true parameter.

code_example:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=[{'role':'user','content':'Hi'}],
    temperature=0.7,
)

title: Embeddings API
short_definition: An API that takes text and returns a fixed-size vector representing its semantic content — used for search, classification, clustering, and recommendation.
full_explanation:
Embeddings APIs are the simplest LLM-adjacent endpoint: send text, get a vector. Fixed-dimension (768-3072 typical), normalized to unit length, suitable for cosine similarity. OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large gives 3072-dim, Cohere embed-v3 gives 1024-dim, BGE-large 1024-dim.

Key design decisions per provider:
- **Single-purpose vs hybrid**: dedicated embedding APIs (OpenAI text-embedding-3) vs models that do both generation and embedding (some open-source).
- **Per-token vs per-call pricing**: embedding APIs typically charge per input token at much lower rates than chat APIs ($0.02-0.13 per 1M tokens for OpenAI/Cohere).
- **Symmetric vs asymmetric**: most embedders use the same model for queries and documents; some use different prefixes ('query: ' vs 'passage: ') for retrieval optimization.
- **Truncation**: models have a max context (typically 8K tokens); inputs longer get truncated.

For RAG, embeddings APIs are called twice in different patterns: once at indexing time to embed all documents (often run as a batch job), and once at query time to embed each user query (real-time). The indexing step dominates cost; the query step dominates latency.
why_it_matters: Every RAG system, semantic search feature, recommendation engine, and clustering pipeline runs on embeddings. The embeddings API is the workhorse of these — what you call to turn text into vectors. Choice of embedding model significantly affects retrieval quality (5-15% difference between weak and strong embedders on standard benchmarks). For practitioners building anything search-adjacent, embedding model choice is a meaningful decision.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of embeddings APIs as a translator from text to coordinates on a giant semantic map. Pass in any text, get back its coordinates. Texts about similar topics land in similar coordinates. Then you can answer 'find similar' or 'cluster by topic' or 'classify' just by computing distances on the map. The translation is lossy but useful — you can't reconstruct the original text from coordinates, but you can find related text efficiently.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: `client.embeddings.create(model='text-embedding-3-large', input=['text 1', 'text 2'])` returns a list of 3072-dim vectors. For batch indexing of many docs, batch the input list (up to 2048 inputs per call) for throughput.

related_terms_explained.connection: Embeddings API is the service that produces embeddings on demand.
Embeddings APIs are foundational infrastructure for RAG systems.
Embeddings APIs implement the bi-encoder retrieval architecture as a service.
Embeddings APIs produce the vectors stored in vector databases.

code_example:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
resp = client.embeddings.create(
    model='text-embedding-3-large',
    input=['Hello world', 'Greetings earthling']
)
vectors = [d.embedding for d in resp.data]

title: gRPC (LLM Serving)
short_definition: A high-performance RPC framework using HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers — used in some LLM serving stacks for lower latency and better streaming than REST.
full_explanation:
Most LLM APIs are JSON-over-HTTP/1.1 (REST). For internal serving inside a cluster — especially for streaming, multiplexing many concurrent requests, or low-latency back-to-back calls — gRPC offers tangible benefits: binary protobuf payloads (smaller, faster to parse), HTTP/2 multiplexing (many calls per connection), and bidirectional streaming.

Usage in LLM stacks: Triton Inference Server uses gRPC as its primary protocol (REST is also supported). TGI (Text Generation Inference) supports gRPC for high-throughput backends. Some agentic frameworks use gRPC internally for tool execution. Public-facing LLM APIs are nearly always REST/SSE because gRPC adds infrastructure complexity that doesn't pay back outside high-traffic internal use.

For application developers calling external LLM APIs, gRPC rarely matters. For platform teams running internal LLM serving at scale or building inference infrastructure, gRPC is one of the tools in the box. The performance difference vs REST/JSON varies — for small, low-frequency calls it's negligible; for batched high-throughput inference, gRPC can shave meaningful percent-tens of latency.
why_it_matters: Niche but worth knowing if you're working on inference infrastructure or building serving platforms. For most application work, REST is fine and gRPC is overkill. Knowing the distinction lets you reason about why some serving stacks have both REST and gRPC endpoints (different consumer needs) and when reaching for gRPC pays off (high-frequency internal calls, latency-sensitive paths).
how_to_think_about_it: Think of REST as English (verbose, universally understood, everyone speaks it) and gRPC as a specialized technical jargon (compact, fast among experts, requires both parties to know it). For talking to the public, English. For technical conversations among specialists, jargon is faster. LLM serving uses English at the edge (REST APIs to customers) and jargon internally (gRPC inside the cluster).
see_it_in_practice: Triton with gRPC: `pip install tritonclient[grpc]; client = tritonclient.grpc.InferenceServerClient('localhost:8001'); response = client.infer('llama', inputs)`. Ships with most NVIDIA inference stacks.

related_terms_explained.connection: gRPC is an alternative to REST APIs for LLM serving.
gRPC has native bidirectional streaming, often used for token-by-token output.
gRPC can deliver higher throughput than REST in high-traffic internal serving.

title: JSON Schema Enforcement
short_definition: An API feature where you provide a JSON schema and the LLM is constrained to produce output matching that schema exactly — guaranteed-parsable structured responses.
full_explanation:
Asking an LLM to 'respond in JSON matching this schema' works most of the time but fails enough to require parse-and-retry logic in production. JSON Schema enforcement makes the guarantee structural rather than instructional. You provide a JSON schema; the API uses constrained decoding (sampling-time logit masking) to ensure every emitted token is valid according to the schema. The output is mathematically certain to parse.

OpenAI's Structured Outputs (`response_format: {type: 'json_schema', json_schema: {...}, strict: true}`) launched 2024 and supports a subset of JSON Schema (no $ref, no recursive definitions, all properties required). Anthropic offers similar via tool-use mode (define a tool with input_schema, the model 'calls' the tool with conformant arguments). Open-source models get this through Outlines, Guidance, or vLLM's structured output mode.

Quality cost: constrained decoding can mildly distort the model's natural distribution, occasionally producing weird outputs to satisfy the schema. The common failure is a model that's forced to produce JSON about a topic it doesn't know — better to fail gracefully than produce confidently-wrong structured data. For production extraction pipelines and tool-calling, the reliability gain is worth the slight quality compromise.
why_it_matters: If you're building production systems that consume LLM output, JSON Schema enforcement is the difference between 'parse-or-retry-with-backoff' and 'always parses, full stop.' Removes a whole class of error handling, makes function calling reliable, and enables more aggressive automation. For practitioners shipping LLM-powered features that involve structured data — extraction, tool calls, classification — this is essentially mandatory infrastructure.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of JSON Schema enforcement like a form with required fields and validation. The model fills out the form; the form's structure is the schema; the system rejects any answer that doesn't fit the form's constraints. The model can still write garbage in the free-text fields, but it can't, say, leave the email field empty or write 'maybe' in a checkbox. Structural validity is guaranteed; semantic correctness still requires good prompting.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: `from pydantic import BaseModel; class User(BaseModel): name: str; age: int; resp = client.beta.chat.completions.parse(model='gpt-4o', messages=[...], response_format=User)`. Returns a parsed Pydantic instance, never a string.

related_terms_explained.connection: JSON Schema enforcement is the strongest form of structured output.
JSON Schema enforcement is implemented via constrained decoding under the hood.
OpenAI's strict-mode flag enables JSON Schema enforcement for tool calls and responses.
Open-source library that brings JSON Schema enforcement to local models.

code_example:
from pydantic import BaseModel
class Person(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int

resp = client.beta.chat.completions.parse(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=[{'role':'user','content':'Extract: John, 32'}],
    response_format=Person,
)
person = resp.choices[0].message.parsed

title: LiteLLM
short_definition: An open-source library that wraps 100+ LLM providers behind a single OpenAI-compatible API — switch between providers without code changes.
full_explanation:
Calling different LLM providers means learning different SDKs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Google, Replicate, Together, etc.) — each with different field names, retry semantics, and error formats. LiteLLM (BerriAI) abstracts all of them behind the OpenAI Chat Completions interface. You write code once; LiteLLM translates to whichever provider's actual API.

Features beyond translation: load balancing across multiple keys/providers, fallback chains (try GPT-4 → Claude on failure), per-call cost tracking, structured logging, prompt caching coordination, embedding API unification, and a proxy server mode that exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint serving any backend. Production deployments use the proxy mode to centralize observability, retry logic, and key management.

Widely adopted in 2024-2026 — most multi-provider LLM teams either use LiteLLM directly or build something LiteLLM-shaped. The alternative — bespoke per-provider integration code — is harder to maintain at scale and harder to A/B test across providers.
why_it_matters: If you're using more than one LLM provider, LiteLLM saves enormous integration effort. If you're using one provider but want flexibility to switch, LiteLLM is cheap insurance — the code remains portable. For platform teams managing LLM access across an organization, LiteLLM Proxy is one of the easiest ways to add centralized rate limiting, cost attribution, and provider failover. Open source, Apache-licensed, easy to adopt.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of LiteLLM as ODBC for LLMs. ODBC was the database adapter layer that let SQL apps work with any database. LiteLLM is the equivalent for LLM APIs — a normalization layer that makes different providers look the same to your application code. The benefit isn't speed; it's portability and operational consistency.
see_it_in_practice: 5 lines: `from litellm import completion; resp = completion(model='gpt-4o', messages=[{'role':'user','content':'hi'}])`. Change model to 'claude-opus-4-7' or 'gemini-2.0-flash' or 'ollama/llama3' — same code, different provider. Proxy mode: `litellm --model gpt-4o --port 8000` exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

related_terms_explained.connection: LiteLLM Proxy mode is one popular implementation of the LLM gateway pattern.
LiteLLM unifies all providers behind the Chat Completions API format.
LiteLLM is an abstraction layer over many LLM provider APIs.
LiteLLM supports automatic fallback chains for cross-provider reliability.

code_example:
from litellm import completion
resp = completion(
    model='claude-opus-4-7',
    messages=[{'role':'user','content':'Hello'}]
)
# Switch model='gpt-4o' or 'gemini-2.0-flash' — same code

title: LLM Gateway
short_definition: A proxy layer that sits between your application and one or more LLM providers — handles authentication, rate limiting, observability, fallback, and cost attribution.
full_explanation:
LLM gateways are reverse proxies specialized for LLM traffic. They expose a single endpoint to your application (typically OpenAI-compatible), then route requests to the right backend (a specific provider, model, or self-hosted deployment) while enforcing operational policy: per-team rate limits, cost budgets, request logging, retry on transient errors, fallback to alternate providers on outage, and prompt caching coordination.

Why a gateway: as an organization grows past a few teams using LLMs, direct provider access creates problems. Each team manages its own API keys (security risk), no central cost visibility (budgeting nightmare), no shared rate limits (one team can monopolize org-wide capacity), no consistent logging (debugging spans tools). A gateway centralizes the operational concerns while letting each team focus on the application logic.

Production options: LiteLLM Proxy (most popular open source), Portkey (managed SaaS + open source), Helicone (observability-first), Cloudflare AI Gateway (edge-deployed), Google Vertex AI Gateway. Most teams adopt one once they're past 3-4 services using LLMs across multiple providers.
why_it_matters: If your organization is scaling LLM usage across teams, gateways are the natural infrastructure layer. They turn 'every team manages their own API keys and learns provider quirks' into 'every team uses our standard endpoint, ops team handles the rest.' For practitioners on platform/infrastructure teams, knowing gateway patterns and tools is essential. For application engineers, knowing 'we're behind a gateway' is useful for debugging routing and auth issues.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of LLM gateways like API gateways for any other service. Web traffic goes through Cloudflare/CloudFront for caching, DDoS protection, routing, observability. LLM traffic increasingly goes through similar gateways for the same operational reasons — caching, cost control, routing, observability. Same architectural pattern, specialized for LLM concerns.
see_it_in_practice: Self-host LiteLLM Proxy: `pip install litellm; litellm --config config.yaml --port 8000`. The config defines models, fallback chains, and per-key rate limits. Application code points at `http://gateway:8000` instead of `https://api.openai.com`.

related_terms_explained.connection: LiteLLM Proxy is the most-used open-source LLM gateway implementation.
LLM gateways sit between application code and provider APIs.
Centralized rate limiting is one of the primary reasons to deploy a gateway.
Gateways implement cross-provider fallback for reliability.

title: MCP (Model Context Protocol)
short_definition: An open protocol from Anthropic for connecting AI assistants to external tools and data sources — standardizes how an LLM client (Claude, IDE, agent) discovers and calls tools.
full_explanation:
Before MCP, every LLM application had to write bespoke integrations to expose tools (databases, APIs, file systems) to the model. The same Slack-tool integration written for ChatGPT had to be rewritten for Claude, then again for Cursor, then again for any new client. MCP (Model Context Protocol, Anthropic, late 2024) standardizes this: tools are exposed as MCP servers; LLM clients implement MCP client. Same server works with any compatible client.

The protocol defines:
- **Servers** that expose tools (functions the LLM can call), resources (data the LLM can read), and prompts (templates with variables).
- **Clients** that connect to servers, list capabilities, invoke tools, and stream results.
- **Transport**: stdio (process-based, fastest for local), HTTP (network, supports multi-tenant), SSE (HTTP with streaming).

Adoption took off in 2025 — Cursor, Claude Desktop, Zed, Continue, and many other dev tools added MCP client support. Hundreds of MCP servers shipped (filesystem, GitHub, Slack, Linear, databases, browser automation). For practitioners building agent infrastructure or new dev tools, MCP is the protocol to integrate against — the network effect of shared servers makes it self-reinforcing.
why_it_matters: MCP is the fastest-adopted protocol in the LLM ecosystem in years. It's solving a real problem (the N×M integration explosion of tools × clients) with a simple, well-designed protocol. For anyone building tools for LLM consumers (data sources, APIs, system integrations), shipping an MCP server makes you usable across every compatible client at once. For anyone building LLM clients (IDEs, chat UIs, agent platforms), implementing MCP client opens up the entire server ecosystem.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of MCP as USB for LLMs. Before USB, every device had its own bespoke connector — keyboard cables, mouse cables, scanner cables, all incompatible. USB standardized the connector so any device works with any computer. MCP does the same for tools and LLM clients — write the integration once, it works everywhere. The standardization unlocks the network effect.
see_it_in_practice: Reference implementations at github.com/modelcontextprotocol. Quick start: `npm install @modelcontextprotocol/sdk`; build a server in 50 lines exposing your tool. Configure Claude Desktop's MCP config to load the server; the tools become available in chat.

related_terms_explained.connection: Servers expose tools, resources, and prompts via MCP.
Clients (LLM applications) connect to MCP servers.
Tools are the most-used MCP capability — functions the LLM can call.
Resources are read-only data accessible via MCP.
MCP standardizes the tool-use protocol across LLM clients.

title: MCP Client
short_definition: An LLM application (Claude Desktop, Cursor, IDE plugin) that knows how to discover, connect to, and call MCP servers — the consumer side of the MCP protocol.
full_explanation:
MCP clients are the LLM-side participants in the protocol. They're typically full applications: Claude Desktop, Cursor, Zed, Continue, OpenAgents — anywhere a user can chat with an LLM and benefit from external tool access. The client's job: load MCP server configurations, spawn or connect to those servers, present their tools/resources/prompts to the model, and execute calls when the model invokes them.

The protocol details: clients discover server capabilities via `tools/list`, `resources/list`, `prompts/list` calls. When the model decides to call a tool, the client routes the invocation to the right server, waits for the result, and feeds it back into the conversation. For resources (read-only data), the client may pre-fetch and include in the model's context.

Client implementations are typically simpler than servers — most of the complexity is in the protocol layer (handled by SDK libraries) plus UI for surfacing tools to users (per-tool permission prompts, server connection management, tool result display).
why_it_matters: If you're building LLM applications — chat UIs, IDE integrations, agent platforms — implementing MCP client support gives users access to the entire MCP server ecosystem. Hundreds of servers ship today; thousands likely by 2027. For application developers, the question isn't 'should I support MCP' but 'when do I add it.'
how_to_think_about_it: Think of MCP clients like web browsers. The browser is the user-facing application; it knows how to speak HTTP and render HTML. It works with any HTTP server because of the standard. MCP clients work with any MCP server for the same reason — speak the protocol, work with any compliant peer.
see_it_in_practice: Add MCP support to your app: `npm install @modelcontextprotocol/sdk` (TypeScript) or equivalent Python/Rust SDK. Client code: connect to server (stdio or HTTP), call `client.listTools()`, surface tools to model, route `tools/call` requests as the model invokes them.

related_terms_explained.connection: MCP client is the consumer-side implementation of the MCP protocol.
Clients connect to servers; the two sides communicate via MCP.
Clients route LLM tool-use calls to MCP servers.
Agent applications often implement MCP client to access external tools.

title: MCP Prompts
short_definition: Prompt templates exposed by MCP servers that LLM clients can discover and let users invoke — like saved prompt presets attached to specific tool integrations.
full_explanation:
MCP servers can expose three things: tools (functions to call), resources (data to read), and prompts (templates the user can invoke). Prompts are template strings with variables that get filled in at invocation. They're discovered via `prompts/list` and invoked via `prompts/get`.

Use case: a Slack MCP server might expose a prompt template called 'summarize-channel' with arguments (channel_name, time_range). The user picks it from the client UI, fills in arguments, and the server returns the populated prompt text — which the client then sends to the LLM. This pattern packages domain expertise (the prompt phrasing that gets best results from this tool's data) alongside the tool integration itself.

Production examples: GitHub MCP server might expose 'release-notes-from-PRs', 'security-review-of-changes', 'changelog-since-version'. Database servers might expose 'investigate-slow-query', 'schema-summary'. Each is a domain-specific prompt template whose authoring lives with the integration, not in every user's prompt library.
why_it_matters: Prompts are the underrated part of MCP. Most attention goes to tools (because they're flashy) but prompts are how integration authors share their hard-won prompt-engineering knowledge with users. For tool authors, including a few well-tuned prompt templates makes the integration immediately useful. For users, MCP prompts mean you don't have to figure out 'how do I phrase a question to get useful output from this tool' — the integration's author already did.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of MCP prompts like recipe cards that come with kitchen equipment. The blender ships with recipes designed for what it does well — smoothies, soups, sauces. You don't have to figure out how to use the blender from scratch; the manufacturer included starter recipes. MCP prompts are the same: shipped with the integration, tuned for what works, ready to use.
see_it_in_practice: In an MCP server: register prompts via `server.setRequestHandler('prompts/list', ...)` and `prompts/get`. Client UI typically shows them in a 'prompt picker' menu. Examples in the modelcontextprotocol example repo demonstrate the pattern.

related_terms_explained.connection: Prompts are one of the three capability types MCP servers can expose.
Servers register and serve prompt templates.
Sister capability — tools execute functions, prompts return templates.
MCP prompts are essentially shareable prompt templates with discovery metadata.

title: MCP Resources
short_definition: Read-only data sources exposed by MCP servers — files, database rows, API responses — that the LLM client can fetch and include in the model's context.
full_explanation:
Where MCP tools are functions the LLM can call (with side effects), resources are data the LLM can read. Each resource has a URI (e.g., `file:///path/to/doc.md`, `slack://channel/general/messages`) and a MIME type. Clients discover resources via `resources/list` and read content via `resources/read`.

The difference from tools matters at the protocol level: resources are explicitly read-only, declarative, and content-typed. A client may safely pre-load resource content into context without user confirmation, where tool calls require explicit invocation. This split lets MCP servers cleanly separate 'data I'm exposing for the model to read' from 'actions the model can take.'

Use cases: a Linear MCP server might expose all open issues as resources (URI like `linear://issue/PRJ-123`). The user attaches an issue to chat; the client reads it via the resource URI and includes the content. A filesystem MCP server exposes file contents as resources. A documentation MCP server exposes pages.
why_it_matters: Resources are how MCP integrations cleanly expose 'data the LLM should read' without requiring tool-call ceremony. For client UIs, resources enable file/data attachment patterns ('drag this issue into chat'). For server authors, resources let you ship integrations that surface data without forcing every read through a tool invocation. Cleaner UX, cleaner protocol semantics.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of resources like the 'attach file' button in chat apps. The user picks a file (or the LLM is given access to it); the content gets included in context. Compared to tools (which are 'click button to do thing'), resources are 'read this thing.' Both are useful; the protocol distinguishes them so clients can apply different UX (resources can autoload; tools require explicit calls).
see_it_in_practice: MCP server: register `resources/list` and `resources/read` handlers. Each resource has a URI, name, description, and MIME type. Clients display them in attachment pickers; reading happens transparently when the resource is referenced.

related_terms_explained.connection: Resources are one of the three capability types MCP servers expose.
Servers register and serve resources.
Sister capability — tools execute, resources read.
Resource content gets loaded into the LLM's context window.

title: MCP Sampling
short_definition: An MCP feature where servers can request the LLM client to run a generation on the server's behalf — lets servers leverage the user's chosen model without managing their own API keys.
full_explanation:
The most clever feature in MCP. Normally, MCP servers expose tools, resources, and prompts to LLM clients. With sampling, the flow inverts: the server can ask the client 'please run this generation for me using your model.' The client (with user permission) runs the generation, returns the result.

Why this matters: it lets MCP servers do LLM-powered work without each server needing its own API key, billing relationship with a provider, or model selection. The user picks one model in their client (Claude Opus, GPT-4, etc.); every MCP server they connect to can use that model on demand.

Example workflow: a 'document analyzer' MCP server gets a doc, wants to summarize one section. It sends `sampling/createMessage` to the client with the prompt. The client (Claude Desktop) sees this, asks the user 'this server wants to use your model for [task] — allow?', runs the call if approved, returns the response.

Sampling shifts cost and policy to the user — the server doesn't pay for the LLM call, the user does (against their existing subscription). This makes deeply-integrated multi-step LLM workflows possible without each integration becoming a paid service.
why_it_matters: Sampling is the feature that lets MCP servers compose LLM-powered workflows without each server needing its own LLM provider relationship. For sophisticated MCP integrations — agents-within-agents, multi-step processing pipelines — sampling is what makes them economically viable. For practitioners building advanced MCP servers, sampling unlocks patterns that would otherwise require server-managed API keys and per-server billing.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine a contractor who comes to your house to do work. Normally they bring their own tools (their own API keys, their own LLM). With sampling, the contractor uses your tools — your hammer, your drill, your saw (your LLM). They don't need to invest in their own toolkit, you don't need to know which contractor uses which brand. The tools belong to you; the contractor just borrows them when needed (with your permission each time).
see_it_in_practice: In MCP server code: `client.createMessage({ messages, maxTokens })` triggers a sampling request. Client UIs typically prompt the user for each sampling call (similar to tool-call prompts). Adoption is growing as more clients add sampling support beyond Claude Desktop.

related_terms_explained.connection: Sampling is an advanced MCP feature for inverted client-server LLM calls.
Servers initiate sampling requests; clients fulfill them.
Clients implement sampling to give servers controlled access to the user's chosen model.

title: MCP Server
short_definition: A program that exposes tools, resources, and prompts to MCP-compatible LLM clients — the producer side of the MCP protocol.
full_explanation:
MCP servers are the supply side. Each server packages an integration (filesystem access, GitHub API wrapper, database connector, browser automation) into a discoverable interface that any MCP client can use. Servers can be implemented in any language with an MCP SDK (TypeScript, Python, Rust, Go) and run as a process the client spawns or as an HTTP service.

A typical server implements:
- **tools/list**: returns list of callable functions with JSON schemas.
- **tools/call**: executes a specific tool with provided arguments.
- **resources/list**: returns available data resources.
- **resources/read**: returns content for a specific resource URI.
- Optional: **prompts/list** and **prompts/get** for shareable prompt templates.

Protocol transport: stdio (subprocess pipe — most common for local dev), HTTP+SSE (network — useful for shared/multi-tenant). Authentication is server-defined — some are open, some require OAuth or API keys.

The MCP server ecosystem has exploded since late 2024. Reference servers exist for filesystem, GitHub, Slack, Linear, Postgres, Puppeteer, Brave Search, and dozens more. Many are 100-500 lines of code.
why_it_matters: If you build tools, services, or data sources you'd like LLMs to access, shipping an MCP server makes you accessible from every MCP-compatible client at once. Compared to writing per-client integrations (Claude plugin, Cursor extension, Zed plugin separately), MCP gives you N-way distribution from one codebase. For builders, MCP server is the right way to expose your service to the LLM ecosystem in 2025-2026.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of MCP servers like web servers. Each server hosts a specific application or service. Web browsers (clients) speak HTTP and can talk to any web server. MCP clients speak MCP and can talk to any MCP server. The standardization means infrastructure (runtimes, frameworks, observability) becomes shared across the ecosystem.
see_it_in_practice: Hello-world MCP server in TypeScript: `import { Server } from '@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server'; const s = new Server({name:'demo', version:'0.1.0'}, {capabilities:{tools:{}}}); s.setRequestHandler('tools/list', () => ({tools:[{name:'echo', description:'echoes input', inputSchema:{...}}]})); s.connect(transport)`. Spawn from Claude Desktop config; tool appears in chat.

related_terms_explained.connection: MCP server is the producer side of the MCP protocol.
Servers serve clients; the two sides communicate via MCP.
Tools are the most common capability type servers expose.
Resources are the read-only data servers can expose.

title: MCP Tools
short_definition: Functions exposed by MCP servers that LLM clients can invoke — read a file, query a database, call an API, control a browser. The most-used MCP capability.
full_explanation:
Tools are the functions an MCP server makes callable by LLM clients. Each tool has a name, description, and JSON schema for its arguments. The LLM (via the client) decides when to call which tool with what arguments; the server executes; the result returns to the LLM as context for further reasoning.

The pattern is identical to OpenAI/Anthropic function calling but standardized across LLM clients. Write the tool once in an MCP server; it works in Claude Desktop, Cursor, Zed, Continue, and any other compatible client. Compare to the pre-MCP world where you'd write separate integrations for each client.

Design considerations for tool authors: descriptive names (the LLM picks tools based on names + descriptions), tight JSON schemas (constrains valid arguments), idempotent where possible (LLMs sometimes retry), error messages that help the LLM (return structured failures it can understand). Good tool design is similar to good API design — clear contracts, self-describing, fail-loud.
why_it_matters: MCP tools are the dominant pattern for adding LLM-callable capabilities to applications in 2025-2026. For anyone building dev tools, agent platforms, or LLM-powered workflows, MCP tools are how you extend what the LLM can do. The shared protocol means your tool integration runs everywhere; the JSON schema enforcement means the LLM can call it reliably. Foundation of modern LLM tool ecosystems.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of MCP tools like the apps installed on your phone. Each app exposes specific capabilities (camera, GPS, payments). The OS standardizes how apps declare and surface capabilities. MCP tools are the same: integrations declare their capabilities; the LLM 'OS' (client + model) calls them as needed. The standardization is what makes the ecosystem work.
see_it_in_practice: Defining a tool in an MCP server: `{name:'get_weather', description:'Get current weather for a city', inputSchema:{type:'object', properties:{city:{type:'string'}}, required:['city']}}`. The LLM sees this in its tool list and can call it like a function.

related_terms_explained.connection: Tools are the most-used MCP capability type.
Servers expose tools; clients invoke them.
MCP tools standardize the underlying tool-use protocol across clients.
MCP tools generalize function calling beyond single-vendor APIs.

title: Messages API (Anthropic)
short_definition: Anthropic's primary API for calling Claude models — similar in spirit to OpenAI's Chat Completions but with richer multimodal content blocks and explicit system-prompt handling.
full_explanation:
Anthropic's Messages API (POST /v1/messages) is the Claude equivalent of Chat Completions. Like OpenAI's API, it takes a list of messages with roles and returns a generated assistant message. Distinguishing features: system prompts are a top-level field (not a message), content can be a list of typed blocks (text, image, document, tool_use, tool_result), and prompt caching is configured per-content-block.

Key fields: `model` (e.g., 'claude-opus-4-7'), `max_tokens` (required, no default), `messages` (alternating user/assistant), `system` (top-level string or list of blocks), `tools`, `tool_choice`, `temperature`, `stream`, `metadata`. Returns `content` (list of blocks), `stop_reason`, `usage` (input/output token counts plus cached counts).

Multimodal support is first-class: text, image, document blocks can be mixed within a single message's content. Anthropic's vision and document-understanding capabilities are accessed through these block types. Tool calling is via tool_use blocks in responses and tool_result blocks in subsequent user messages.

The API is generally considered well-designed by practitioners — explicit content typing, clean prompt caching integration, and consistent tool-use structure. Adoption is growing as Claude models become more popular for production deployments.
why_it_matters: If you're using Claude in production, Messages API is the foundational interface. Knowing the content-block structure, prompt caching, and tool-use conventions is essential for building anything beyond basic chat. The API is more nuanced than Chat Completions — knowing where to use blocks vs strings, where to set cache_control, how to structure tool results — pays off in cleaner code and lower bills.
how_to_think_about_it: Where Chat Completions is a flat 'list of message strings,' Messages API is more like 'list of typed content blocks per message.' The flexibility costs slightly more learning but enables features (vision, documents, prompt caching) that benefit from typed structure. Think of it as JSON's typed objects vs flat key-value strings — more expressive once you learn it.
see_it_in_practice: Hello world: `from anthropic import Anthropic; client = Anthropic(); resp = client.messages.create(model='claude-opus-4-7', max_tokens=1024, messages=[{'role':'user','content':'Hello'}])`. Multimodal: `content=[{'type':'image','source':{...}},{'type':'text','text':'describe this'}]`.

related_terms_explained.connection: OpenAI's parallel API; structurally similar but with different field names and semantics.
Configured via cache_control on Messages API content blocks.
Messages API supports tool use via typed tool_use and tool_result blocks.
Messages API is Anthropic's primary LLM API surface.

code_example:
from anthropic import Anthropic
client = Anthropic()
resp = client.messages.create(
    model='claude-opus-4-7',
    max_tokens=1024,
    system='You are concise',
    messages=[{'role':'user','content':'Hi'}],
)

title: OpenAI Batch API (Reprise)
short_definition: OpenAI's batch processing API — submit thousands of requests in a single JSONL upload, get results within 24h at 50% off real-time pricing.
full_explanation:
OpenAI's specific implementation of batch APIs (covered generally in batch-api). Distinguishing details: input is a JSONL file uploaded via the Files API, each line a complete request body for chat completions or embeddings. The batch is created with `client.batches.create()`, processed asynchronously, and results are returned as another JSONL file when complete.

Limits: 50K requests per batch, up to 90M tokens total per batch. Rate-limit-wise, batch usage doesn't count against your real-time TPM/RPM — separate quotas. Completion typically much faster than the 24h SLA for small batches; large batches genuinely use the full window.

Good fits: synthetic data generation for fine-tuning (millions of cheap inferences), one-shot dataset processing (classify all support tickets from last quarter), evaluation runs (score thousands of model outputs against gold answers), backfill operations on accumulated data.

Billing and billing only: 50% off both input and output rates. The discount is essentially universal — there's no quality difference, just async timing. For any workload that doesn't need real-time response, the batch API is the economically obvious choice.
why_it_matters: If you have data-engineering or evaluation workloads using OpenAI APIs and aren't using the batch API, you're paying twice as much as you should. The 5-minute integration cost recovers in your first sizable job. For practitioners running offline LLM workflows on OpenAI infrastructure, this is essential cost-saving knowledge.
how_to_think_about_it: Same mental model as the general batch-api entry — overnight shipping vs same-day. Same package, same destination, half the price for 24h tolerance. For non-urgent OpenAI workloads, batch is the rational default.
see_it_in_practice: Construct JSONL: each line `{custom_id: '...', method: 'POST', url: '/v1/chat/completions', body: {...}}`. Upload via files API. Create batch. Poll `client.batches.retrieve(batch.id)` until 'completed'. Download results via files API.

related_terms_explained.connection: OpenAI Batch is one specific implementation of the batch-API pattern.
Batch API offers 50% off both input and output token rates.
Batch processing wraps the same Chat Completions endpoint with async batching.

title: OpenAPI Tool Binding
short_definition: Auto-generating LLM tool definitions from existing OpenAPI specifications — turn any documented REST API into LLM-callable tools without writing per-endpoint code.
full_explanation:
Most existing services have OpenAPI (formerly Swagger) specs documenting their endpoints, parameters, and response shapes. OpenAPI tool binding parses those specs and produces LLM tool definitions automatically — the LLM gets one tool per endpoint, with arguments mapped from OpenAPI parameter schemas to function parameters.

The immediate win: any service with a published OpenAPI spec can become LLM-callable in minutes instead of writing dozens of bespoke tool wrappers. Stripe, Slack, GitHub, AWS, virtually every modern SaaS — all publish OpenAPI specs. Wrap them once with a binding library and the LLM can read invoices, post messages, list issues, manage cloud resources.

Libraries: LangChain's OpenAPI toolkits, LlamaIndex's openapi tool spec, Microsoft's TypeChat, OpenAI's function-calling cookbook patterns. The MCP filesystem reference server doesn't use this pattern but many community MCP servers wrap OpenAPI specs to expose third-party APIs as MCP tools.

Limits: large APIs with hundreds of endpoints overwhelm the LLM's tool-selection ability. Production systems usually filter to a relevant subset (e.g., only Stripe's billing endpoints, not the entire surface). And not every OpenAPI spec is correct or complete enough for direct LLM use — some need cleanup.
why_it_matters: If you want to give an LLM access to existing services, OpenAPI binding is dramatically faster than writing per-endpoint tool code. For any team building LLM agents on top of internal microservices (which often already have OpenAPI specs), this pattern saves weeks of integration work. For agents calling external services, OpenAPI binding gives you instant access to the ecosystem of well-documented APIs.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of OpenAPI specs as machine-readable API documentation. Tool binding is the compiler that turns documentation into LLM-callable functions. You wrote the docs once (or someone else did); the binding gets you everything callable for free. The alternative — hand-writing tool wrappers per endpoint — is what you should avoid when an OpenAPI spec already exists.
see_it_in_practice: LangChain: `from langchain.agents.agent_toolkits.openapi import planner; toolkit = planner.create_openapi_agent(spec_path, requests_wrapper, llm)`. The agent gets all spec endpoints as tools. Filter by tag or path prefix to limit scope.

related_terms_explained.connection: OpenAPI binding generates standard tool-use tool definitions.
Each OpenAPI endpoint becomes a function-callable tool.
Agents commonly use OpenAPI binding to access external services without bespoke integration code.
Many MCP servers wrap OpenAPI specs to expose third-party APIs.

title: Function Calling (Parallel)
short_definition: An API mode where the LLM can output multiple tool calls in a single response, executed in parallel, instead of one-at-a-time sequential calls.
full_explanation:
Standard function calling produces one tool call per response. The LLM picks one tool, your code runs it, you feed the result back, the LLM picks the next. For independent calls (look up weather in 5 cities), this serializes work that could parallelize.

Parallel function calling lets the LLM emit a list of tool_calls in one response. Your application runs them concurrently and feeds all results back as a single multi-result message. For independent operations, this collapses N round-trips of latency into 1.

OpenAI introduced this in late 2023; Anthropic followed in 2024. The model decides whether to use parallel calls based on whether the tools' outputs depend on each other. 'Get weather in NYC AND get weather in LA' usually parallelizes; 'get user ID THEN look up that user's orders' usually doesn't (the second call depends on the first).

Production impact: agent loops that involve multiple independent lookups (e.g., 'gather context from N sources before answering') become noticeably faster. For agents doing many sequential operations, the wins compound. For simple chat with one tool call per turn, parallel doesn't matter.
why_it_matters: If you're building agents that gather context from multiple sources or perform multiple independent operations per turn, parallel function calling cuts latency significantly. The change is mostly server-side; your application code just needs to handle a list of tool_calls instead of one. For practitioners building production agents, knowing this exists and structuring tools to allow parallelism (independent inputs/outputs) is a real performance lever.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of it like a chef batching kitchen operations. Sequentially: chop onions, then chop carrots, then chop celery. Parallel: assign three sous-chefs to chop simultaneously. Same total work, but completed in 1/3 the wall clock time. Function calls work the same — independent ones can run in parallel; dependent ones must serialize. The model decides which is which.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: tool_calls in response is a list, not a single call. Your code: `for tc in resp.choices[0].message.tool_calls: results.append(execute_tool(tc.function.name, tc.function.arguments))`. Run with asyncio.gather for true concurrency.

related_terms_explained.connection: Parallel function calling is an extension of standard tool-use protocol.
Same concept; parallel adds simultaneous execution of multiple calls.
Agents benefit most from parallel calling since they often gather context from many sources.

title: Prompt Caching (API)
short_definition: An API feature where repeated prompt prefixes are cached server-side, charged at 50-90% discount, and retrieved with much lower latency than fresh processing.
full_explanation:
Provider-side prompt caching reduces cost and latency on repeated context. The provider maintains a cache of recently-seen prompt prefixes; subsequent requests hitting the same prefix get served from cache for the cached portion at a steep discount. Anthropic's implementation requires explicit cache_control markers; OpenAI's auto-detects (no opt-in needed).

The economic case: a 10K-token system prompt reused across 100 user queries costs ~$300 with naive serving (10K * 100 * $3/M = $3.00/M); with prompt caching at 90% discount on cached tokens, it costs $0.30/M. Plus the cached portion processes faster — typically 2-5x latency reduction on the cached prefix. For RAG with stable retrieved context, agent loops with fixed instructions, or any workload with repeated long prefixes, this is essentially free money.

Key implementation details: cache TTL (typically 5 minutes for ephemeral, longer with explicit configuration), cache write cost (small surcharge to populate cache, usually 25%), cache read discount (90% on Anthropic, 50% on OpenAI). For the savings to materialize, the same prefix must be reused at least 1-2 times within the TTL window.

Adoption is universal among major providers in 2025-2026 because the use cases (RAG, agents, batch evals) are universal. Most apps that should be using it aren't — leaving 50-80% cost reduction on the table.
why_it_matters: If your app has any repeated long context — system prompts, RAG context windows, agent instructions, evaluation rubrics — prompt caching is the highest-ROI optimization available. 5-minute integration; massive recurring savings. For practitioners shipping LLM apps, knowing exactly which prefixes to cache and for how long is mandatory cost-engineering literacy.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of prompt caching like browser caching for static assets. The first time someone visits your site, they download all the CSS, JS, images. Subsequent visits, the browser pulls from cache — fast and free. Prompt caching is the same for LLM context: pay full price the first time, near-free on subsequent uses while the cache is warm. The optimization is identifying which 'static assets' (prompt prefixes) get reused enough to benefit.
see_it_in_practice: Anthropic explicit: cache_control on content blocks (covered in anthropic-prompt-caching). OpenAI implicit: long prompts (>1024 tokens) auto-cached for the next 5-10 minutes — no API change needed. Verify cache hits via `usage.prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens` in OpenAI responses.

related_terms_explained.connection: Anthropic's specific implementation of prompt caching.
Prompt caching is the highest-impact LLM cost-reduction feature.
RAG with stable retrieved context is the canonical prompt-caching use case.
Prompt caching reduces effective cost of using long context windows.

title: Rate Limit (LLM APIs)
short_definition: Provider-enforced caps on how many requests or tokens you can send per minute — varies by model and account tier, throttles or rejects excess traffic.
full_explanation:
All LLM APIs enforce rate limits. The two common dimensions: requests per minute (RPM) and tokens per minute (TPM, often split into input and output). When you exceed either, the API returns 429 Too Many Requests; well-behaved clients back off and retry.

Limits vary dramatically by provider, model, and your spending tier. OpenAI tier 1 (after $5 spent) gets ~3,500 RPM and 10K TPM on GPT-4o; tier 5 (after $1000+ spent) gets 30,000 RPM and 800K TPM. Anthropic's tiers progress similarly. Self-hosted serving has effectively no rate limits — bottleneck is GPU capacity.

Production patterns:
- **Exponential backoff**: retry 429s with increasing delay. Standard library in most LLM SDKs.
- **Token bucket**: client-side counting to stay under limits proactively.
- **Concurrent connection limits**: cap parallel requests below the provider's limit minus headroom.
- **Multi-key rotation**: shard load across multiple API keys (within ToS).
- **Multi-provider failover**: route to alternate providers if one is rate-limited.

LLM gateways centralize rate-limit handling so application code doesn't have to. For high-traffic apps, dedicated rate-limit infrastructure is essential.
why_it_matters: Hit rate limits in production and your features break. Knowing your tier limits, designing for them, and building proper backoff/retry/fallback are mandatory skills for production LLM ops. Most teams discover rate limits the hard way during launch traffic spikes; the right time to architect for them is before.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of rate limits like highway speed limits. Drive under and you're fine; drive over and you get stopped. The 'speed limit' (your tier's RPM/TPM) is the contract; staying within it is your responsibility. For sustained high traffic, you upgrade tiers (apply for higher limits, increase spend) the way you'd take a faster road. Fighting the speed limit (retrying without backoff) just gets you ticketed harder.
see_it_in_practice: Most SDKs auto-retry 429s with exponential backoff (OpenAI Python: `client = OpenAI(max_retries=3)`). For high-traffic apps, monitor response headers `x-ratelimit-remaining-requests` and `x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens` to predict and avoid hitting limits.

related_terms_explained.connection: Rate limits are enforced at the API layer; every API has them.
Gateways centralize rate-limit handling for cross-team apps.
Multi-provider fallback is one strategy for handling rate-limit exhaustion.

title: Realtime API (OpenAI)
short_definition: OpenAI's WebSocket-based API for real-time, bidirectional voice and text streaming with GPT-4o — used for voice assistants and low-latency conversational apps.
full_explanation:
Standard chat APIs are request/response over HTTP. Real-time conversational interfaces — voice assistants, live captioning, simultaneous translation — need bidirectional streaming with sub-second latency. OpenAI's Realtime API (released 2024) provides this via WebSocket: the client streams audio chunks in, GPT-4o emits audio chunks (and optional text) out, both directions concurrent.

Distinguishing features: native audio in/out (no separate STT + LLM + TTS pipeline), function calling support inline with audio, configurable voice (multiple options), interruption handling (user can talk over the model), and turn detection (server detects when user stops speaking). Latency from speech end to first audio response is typically 200-500ms vs 1500-3000ms for piped STT→LLM→TTS architectures.

Use cases: voice assistants in cars, customer-support voice bots, language tutors, accessibility tools, streaming interpreters. The API is more complex to integrate than Chat Completions (WebSocket state, audio chunk formats, server events) but the latency improvement is dramatic.
why_it_matters: If you're building voice-first products, the Realtime API offers latency that makes natural conversation feel possible — close to talking to a person. The architectural shift (single API vs three-stage pipeline) also reduces complexity and points of failure. For practitioners building voice agents, customer-support bots, or accessibility tools, knowing the Realtime API exists and what it enables is foundational.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of standard chat APIs like email — send a message, wait for reply, send next message. Realtime API is like a phone call — both sides talk simultaneously, can interrupt, can hear pauses. For phone-call-like use cases (voice assistants), email semantics are unworkable. Realtime is the right architecture for the use case.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI Realtime SDK: connect via WebSocket to `wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime?model=gpt-4o-realtime-preview`, send `session.update` with config, then stream audio frames. SDK examples in openai/openai-realtime-* repos.

related_terms_explained.connection: Realtime API extends streaming to bidirectional with audio first-class.
Realtime API is built on WebSockets for full-duplex streaming.
Realtime API powers voice-mode features in chat applications.
Realtime API supports function calling inline with audio interaction.

title: Responses API (OpenAI)
short_definition: OpenAI's newer unified API that combines features of Chat Completions and Assistants — stateful conversation, built-in tools, and structured output in one endpoint.
full_explanation:
Released in early 2025, the Responses API is OpenAI's attempt to consolidate two separate APIs (Chat Completions and Assistants) into one cleaner interface. Like Assistants, it supports stateful conversations and built-in tools (web search, file search, code interpreter). Like Chat Completions, the request/response semantics are direct without the threads/runs ceremony.

Key design decisions:
- **Conversations are first-class**: pass `previous_response_id` to continue a conversation; OpenAI maintains state.
- **Built-in tools**: web_search_preview, file_search, computer_use, code_interpreter — call them via tool config without bespoke implementation.
- **Reasoning support**: when using o1/o3-class models, the API exposes the reasoning token budget and step counts.
- **Backward compat**: response shape is similar enough to Chat Completions that migration is mostly mechanical.

New projects in 2025+ are increasingly starting on Responses API instead of Chat Completions. Anthropic and Google have similar consolidation movements (different naming conventions). The trend: APIs are getting richer to handle agent-like workloads natively rather than requiring application code to manage state and tool execution.
why_it_matters: If you're starting a new OpenAI integration in 2025-2026, Responses API is the forward-looking choice. The built-in tools (especially web_search and file_search) save significant integration work. For projects that don't need the cross-provider portability of Chat Completions, Responses unlocks features Chat Completions doesn't have. Worth understanding when picking API surface for new projects.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of Chat Completions as the simple HTTP API that wins on portability. Assistants was the higher-level managed API that won on features but lost on lock-in. Responses is OpenAI's answer to 'can we have both?' — managed conveniences with simpler request semantics. Whether the bet on more-built-in-features pays off depends on whether you value the convenience or the portability more.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: `client.responses.create(model='gpt-4o', input='What's new in AI?', tools=[{'type':'web_search_preview'}])`. The model can decide to call web_search internally, get results, and respond — all in one API call.

related_terms_explained.connection: The simpler predecessor; Responses adds built-in tools and state management.
The more complex predecessor; Responses simplifies the threads/runs model.
Responses API includes built-in tools for web search, file search, code interpreter.
Reasoning model support is a Responses API feature.

title: Responses API Reasoning (OpenAI)
short_definition: Responses API support for o-series reasoning models (o1, o3) — exposes the reasoning token budget, step counts, and internal-thinking metadata.
full_explanation:
OpenAI's o-series models (o1, o3) reason internally before producing visible output. Standard Chat Completions hides this — you see only the final response. Responses API surfaces the reasoning structure: how many internal tokens were used, what reasoning steps occurred, and (with verbosity controls) summaries of the thinking.

Key fields exposed: `usage.output_tokens_details.reasoning_tokens` (count of internal thinking tokens billed separately), `reasoning.effort` (control level: 'low', 'medium', 'high' — trades cost for thoroughness), `reasoning.summary` (optional textual summary of the model's reasoning steps).

The billing implication: reasoning tokens are charged at output rate but invisible in the visible response. A query that produces 100 visible tokens might use 8000 reasoning tokens, billing as 8100 output tokens. Understanding this is critical for cost modeling on reasoning models — the ratio of reasoning to visible tokens varies wildly per query.

Production usage: for hard reasoning tasks (math, code, multi-step logic), reasoning models with reasoning.effort='high' often outperform any non-reasoning model. For simple tasks, they're overpaying — route easy queries to gpt-4o-mini, only hard ones to o3.
why_it_matters: Reasoning models are the dominant 2024-2026 pattern for hard tasks. The Responses API is how you use them effectively from OpenAI. Knowing the reasoning_tokens billing dimension prevents surprise bills; knowing the effort levels lets you tune cost-vs-quality on reasoning workloads.
