For practitioners using OpenAI's o-series, this is mandatory operational knowledge.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of reasoning models as a contractor who bills both for hours of thought and for the deliverable. Standard models bill only for the deliverable. Reasoning effort is how much time the contractor is allowed to think before delivering. Higher effort = better deliverable, more total bill. Pick the effort level matching the task's importance.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: `client.responses.create(model='o3', input=hard_problem, reasoning={'effort':'high'})`. Check `resp.usage.output_tokens_details.reasoning_tokens` to see how many thinking tokens were used (and billed).

related_terms_explained.connection: Reasoning support is a Responses API feature for o-series models.
Reasoning token budgets are the underlying control mechanism.
Reasoning models internalize chain-of-thought; the API exposes its metadata.
OpenAI's first reasoning model; Responses API adds support for o1, o3, and successors.

title: Server-Sent Events (SSE)
short_definition: A simple HTTP-based protocol for one-way streaming from server to client — the standard wire format for LLM token streaming over Chat Completions APIs.
full_explanation:
Server-Sent Events (SSE) is a lightweight protocol where the server keeps an HTTP connection open and pushes text events as they happen. Each event is a line starting with `data:` followed by content. The client parses the stream incrementally, processing each event as it arrives. Browsers implement EventSource for native SSE support; servers just need to keep the connection open and emit `data:` lines.

For LLMs, SSE is how token streaming works over standard HTTP. Set `stream=true` in your request; the server returns Content-Type `text/event-stream` and emits one event per generated token (or chunk). Each event typically contains a JSON delta with the new content. The stream terminates with a special `data: [DONE]` event.

Benefits over WebSockets for this use case: simpler (HTTP-based, works through proxies and corporate firewalls without special handling), cacheable headers, native browser support without library, and stateless server side. WebSockets win for full-duplex streaming (Realtime API for voice); SSE wins for one-way token streaming.

Virtually every chat API supports SSE streaming: OpenAI, Anthropic, vLLM, Ollama, and most others. The wire format is consistent enough that streaming code from one provider often works against another with minimal changes.
why_it_matters: If you're building chat UIs or any application that should display LLM output as it generates, SSE is the standard way to get the streaming effect. Understanding how it works at the HTTP level lets you debug streaming issues (proxies eating events, buffering problems, encoding mismatches). For practitioners building any LLM-facing UI, SSE literacy is foundational.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of SSE like a one-way radio broadcast. The server is the broadcaster, the client is the listener. As long as the radio is on, the client hears whatever the server says. Compared to WebSockets (a two-way phone call), SSE is simpler — you just need to listen, not respond. For LLM token streaming, you don't need to talk back during the stream, so SSE is the right tool.
see_it_in_practice: Browser: `const es = new EventSource(url); es.onmessage = (e) => console.log(JSON.parse(e.data))`. Server (Express): `res.setHeader('Content-Type','text/event-stream'); res.write('data: ' + JSON.stringify(chunk) + '\n\n')`. Most LLM SDKs handle SSE under the hood when you pass `stream=true`.

related_terms_explained.connection: SSE is the standard transport for LLM token streaming over HTTP.
Chat Completions returns SSE when stream=true is passed.
WebSockets are the alternative; SSE is one-way, WebSockets are bidirectional.

title: Streaming (Token Streaming)
short_definition: Returning LLM output incrementally as each token is generated, instead of waiting for the full response — gives the user immediate feedback while generation continues.
full_explanation:
Without streaming, an LLM API call generates the full response server-side, then returns. For long outputs, the user waits 2-30 seconds staring at a loading spinner. With streaming, tokens flow back as they're generated — the first token typically appears in 200-500ms, and the full response builds up at hundreds of tokens per second.

Mechanically: client sends request with `stream=true`. Server returns SSE (Server-Sent Events) over HTTP, emitting one event per token (or token chunk for efficiency). Each event contains a JSON delta with the new content; the client appends to the displayed response in real time.

Streaming improves perceived latency dramatically. Time-to-first-token (TTFT) becomes the user-felt latency, replacing total response time. For chat applications, this is the difference between 'feels slow' and 'feels responsive.' For long generations (essays, code, summaries), it's the difference between 'feels broken' and 'feels alive.'

Downsides: more complex client code (handle deltas, parse JSON streams, manage UI updates), no easy retry on partial failure (once tokens have streamed, you can't 'unstream'), and metrics like token usage are only available at stream end.
why_it_matters: If you're building any user-facing LLM UI and not streaming, you're shipping a worse experience than necessary. Streaming is one of the highest-impact UX changes in LLM development — same model, same answer, dramatically better perceived speed. For practitioners building chat, copilot, or agentic UIs, streaming is essentially mandatory.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of streaming like watching someone type a response in real-time vs them sending you a finished email. The email might be objectively faster (no typing pauses), but watching the typing feels more responsive — you start reading immediately, you can stop them mid-sentence if you've changed your mind, you don't stare at a 'someone is typing...' indicator forever. Streaming applies the same psychology to LLM output.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: `for chunk in client.chat.completions.create(..., stream=True): print(chunk.choices[0].delta.content or '', end='', flush=True)`. Anthropic: `with client.messages.stream(...) as stream: for text in stream.text_stream: print(text, end='', flush=True)`.

related_terms_explained.connection: SSE is the standard wire format for LLM token streaming.
Time-to-first-token is the latency metric streaming optimizes.
WebSockets are an alternative for streaming; SSE is more common for chat.
Chat Completions APIs all support streaming via stream=true parameter.

title: Strict Mode (OpenAI)
short_definition: OpenAI's flag that enables true JSON Schema enforcement on tool calls and structured outputs — guaranteed-conformant outputs instead of best-effort.
full_explanation:
Standard OpenAI tool calling and JSON-mode outputs are best-effort: the model is prompted to follow the schema, usually does, sometimes doesn't. Strict mode (`strict: true`) flips the guarantee. Under the hood, OpenAI runs constrained decoding that mathematically prevents any non-conforming token. The output is guaranteed to parse against the schema, every time.

Limitations of strict mode (compared to fully arbitrary JSON Schema): no $ref or recursive types, all properties must be required, additionalProperties must be false. These restrictions exist because the constrained-decoding implementation needs a finite, non-recursive grammar. Most practical schemas can be rewritten to fit; deeply recursive ones (trees, linked lists) can't be expressed natively.

When to use: production extraction where parse failures aren't acceptable, function-calling where the LLM's tool arguments must always be valid, agent loops where downstream code can't tolerate malformed JSON. The performance cost is negligible (constrained decoding is fast); the reliability gain is dramatic.

Available on chat completions (`response_format: {type:'json_schema', json_schema: {...}, strict: true}`) and tool calls (`tool: {type:'function', function: {name:..., parameters:..., strict: true}}`).
why_it_matters: If you're building production LLM features that consume structured output, enabling strict mode (where supported) removes a whole class of error handling. The investment is one additional flag; the payoff is no more JSON-parse retries. For any team building extraction, function-calling, or agent loops on OpenAI, strict mode should be the default unless you have a specific reason otherwise.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of strict mode like a contract with teeth. Without strict, the model agrees to follow your schema 'in good faith' — usually does, sometimes doesn't. With strict, the model is structurally prevented from violating the schema — not by goodwill, but by sampling-time enforcement. Different tier of guarantee for the cost of one boolean flag.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI tool with strict: `tools=[{'type':'function','function':{'name':'get_weather','description':'...','parameters':{type:'object',properties:{city:{type:'string'}},required:['city'],additionalProperties:False},'strict':True}]`. Output guaranteed to parse against the schema.

related_terms_explained.connection: Strict mode is OpenAI's implementation of JSON Schema enforcement.
Strict is the strongest form of structured output guarantee.
Strict mode uses constrained decoding under the hood.
Strict mode applies to function calling for guaranteed-valid tool arguments.

title: Structured Output (JSON Mode)
short_definition: An LLM API mode where the response is constrained to be valid JSON matching some schema — for reliable parsing in downstream code.
full_explanation:
Many LLM applications need structured data, not free-form text: extracting fields from documents, classifying inputs, generating function arguments, returning lists of items. 'Structured output' is the umbrella term for API features that produce parseable structured responses.

Three levels of structured output:
1. **JSON mode** (lightest): server prompts model to produce valid JSON. Output is guaranteed parseable but not schema-conformant. OpenAI's response_format='json_object'.
2. **JSON Schema mode**: server constrains output to match a user-provided schema. Stricter; may use constrained decoding. OpenAI's response_format='json_schema' with strict:true is the strongest.
3. **Tool calling**: model 'calls' a tool whose input_schema serves as the structure. The arguments are returned as conformant JSON.

Production usage: use the strongest available form for your provider/model combination. For OpenAI, JSON Schema with strict:true. For Anthropic, tool calling with input_schema (which is enforced). For local models, Outlines or Guidance for FSM-based enforcement.

The quality consideration: aggressive schema enforcement can occasionally distort the model's output (forcing it to fill required fields with low-quality content). Best practice: keep schemas minimal and let the model focus on getting the actual content right.
why_it_matters: Structured output is what makes LLMs usable in production pipelines. Free-text output requires brittle parsing (regex, prompt engineering 'please respond in this format'). Structured output makes downstream code reliable. For any team building LLM features that consume model output programmatically, mastering structured output is essential.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of structured output as the difference between a freelance writer turning in an essay vs filling out a form. The essay is harder to process; the form has known fields you can extract automatically. Structured output is your insistence on the form. The LLM still does the writing; you control the format.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI Pydantic example: `class Person(BaseModel): name: str; age: int; resp = client.beta.chat.completions.parse(model='gpt-4o', messages=msgs, response_format=Person)`. `resp.choices[0].message.parsed` is a Person instance, never a parsing error.

related_terms_explained.connection: JSON Schema is the strongest form of structured output.
OpenAI's strict mode enables true schema enforcement on structured outputs.
Structured output is implemented via constrained decoding for the strongest guarantees.
Function/tool calling is a form of structured output where the schema is the tool signature.
Open-source library for structured output on local models.

title: Tool Choice
short_definition: A parameter that controls whether and which tool the LLM is allowed to call — auto, none, required, or a specific tool.
full_explanation:
By default, the model decides whether to use any tool, which one, with what arguments. Sometimes you need more control: force the model to use a specific tool, prevent any tool use, or guarantee that some tool is called. The tool_choice parameter handles these.

Values across providers (OpenAI/Anthropic syntax similar):
- **`auto`** (default): model picks if/which tool to use. Standard behavior.
- **`none`**: model is forbidden from using tools. Useful when you've added tools but want a pure-text answer for this turn.
- **`required`** (OpenAI) / `any` (Anthropic): model must call some tool — picks which one but can't reply with text only.
- **`{type: 'function', function: {name: 'X'}}`**: model must call tool X specifically.

Use cases: a classification step that must always emit a structured tool call (use required + a single classification tool); a summarization step that should never call tools (use none); an extraction pipeline where you need to force a specific extractor (use the specific tool form). Without tool_choice control, you'd rely on prompt engineering ('please call the X tool'), which is less reliable.
why_it_matters: If you're building anything beyond basic chat with tools, tool_choice is a precision instrument for forcing specific behavior. Eliminates prompt-engineering ceremony for control flow. For practitioners building structured agent workflows, classification pipelines, or multi-step extraction, knowing the tool_choice options is essential infrastructure.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of tool_choice like the difference between 'you can use any tool you want' (auto), 'use this specific hammer' (specific tool), 'you must use a tool from this set' (required), or 'no tools at all this time' (none). The hammer-pointing version is most useful for pipelines with specific stages; the others handle edge cases of always-tool and never-tool.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: `client.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-4o', messages=msgs, tools=tools, tool_choice={'type':'function','function':{'name':'classify'}})` forces the model to call the classify function specifically.

related_terms_explained.connection: tool_choice is the control parameter for tool-use behavior.
tool_choice controls function calling at fine granularity.
tool_choice can force a specific tool but doesn't override parallel calling.

title: Tool Use (Function Calling)
short_definition: The API pattern where you give the LLM definitions of functions it can call, the model produces structured calls to those functions, and your code executes them and returns results.
full_explanation:
Tool use (also called function calling) is how LLMs interact with the outside world — databases, APIs, file systems, anything beyond text. The API contract: provide a list of available tools (each with name, description, JSON schema for arguments). The model decides whether to call any tool and with what arguments, returning a structured tool_call instead of text. Your code runs the actual tool function, returns the result, and the model continues the conversation with that context.

The loop:
1. Provide messages + tool definitions to model.
2. Model returns either text response (no tool needed) or tool_calls (one or more).
3. Application executes each tool call against real systems.
4. Application appends tool results to messages, calls model again.
5. Model produces final response based on tool outputs.

This loop is the foundation of agents. A simple chat with tools = single-iteration tool use. An agent = multiple iterations with the model deciding to call tools, see results, decide to call more tools, etc., until done.

Provider differences: OpenAI uses 'tools' array in request, returns tool_calls in response, expects tool_call_id when feeding results back. Anthropic uses similar structure with content blocks (tool_use blocks in response, tool_result blocks in next user message). Functionally equivalent; syntax differs.
why_it_matters: Tool use is the most important API pattern in modern LLM development after basic chat. Every agent system, every LLM-powered automation, every assistant that does anything beyond conversation — all built on tool use. For practitioners building anything beyond text-in-text-out, tool use literacy is the foundation. Foundational concept for the entire agent ecosystem.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of the LLM as a brain in a jar. Without tools, it can only think and produce text. Tools are the limbs, eyes, and voice — they let the brain reach into the world to read databases, write files, send emails, take actions. The LLM writes the function call (the intent); your code runs the function (the action); the result flows back to the brain to inform the next thought. Tool use is the API by which the brain controls its body.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: define tool with JSON schema, pass in tools=[...]; check resp.choices[0].message.tool_calls for any calls; execute, append tool result message with tool_call_id, call API again. Anthropic Messages API has a similar tool_use / tool_result block pattern.

related_terms_explained.connection: Function calling is OpenAI's name for tool use; same concept.
tool_choice parameter controls which tool the model is allowed to call.
Agents are built on iterated tool-use loops.
MCP standardizes tool-use across LLM clients.
Parallel calling extends tool use to multiple simultaneous calls.

code_example:
tools = [{'type':'function','function':{'name':'get_weather','description':'Get weather','parameters':{...}}}]
resp = client.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-4o', messages=msgs, tools=tools)
for tc in resp.choices[0].message.tool_calls:
    result = execute(tc.function.name, json.loads(tc.function.arguments))

title: Webhook (LLM Integrations)
short_definition: An HTTP callback URL where an external service POSTs data when an event occurs — the standard pattern for LLM apps to receive real-time updates from third parties.
full_explanation:
Webhooks are a simple integration pattern: you register a URL with a third-party service; when something happens there (new email, new GitHub PR, new Slack message), they POST a JSON payload to your URL. Your application processes the event, optionally calls an LLM, and takes action. Webhooks are the inverse of polling — push instead of pull.

For LLM applications, webhooks enable event-driven workflows: 'when a new support ticket arrives, classify and route'; 'when a PR is opened, run an LLM code review'; 'when a customer sends a Slack message, generate a response draft.' The webhook is the trigger; the LLM call is the action.

Practical considerations:
- **Verification**: webhooks must verify request signatures (HMAC) to prevent forgery.
- **Idempotency**: providers may retry; your handler must handle duplicate deliveries safely.
- **Latency budget**: most providers expect <5-10 second response. Long LLM calls should be queued/async-processed.
- **Retry handling**: respond with 5xx for transient errors (provider retries); 4xx for permanent errors (provider gives up).

Third-party webhooks (GitHub, Stripe, Slack, etc.) are the most common, but LLM providers themselves expose webhooks too — OpenAI's Batch API can webhook on completion; some Anthropic features signal via webhooks.
why_it_matters: If you're building event-driven LLM features (anything that responds to changes elsewhere), webhooks are the standard wire-up. Knowing how to receive, verify, queue, and respond to webhooks correctly is foundational integration plumbing. For practitioners building real-world LLM apps that talk to external services, webhook literacy is unavoidable.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of webhooks like an answering service. Instead of you constantly calling someone to ask 'anything new?', they call you when something happens. Webhooks are the same: external services call your URL when events occur, you handle them as they come. More efficient than polling, more responsive too.
see_it_in_practice: Express handler: `app.post('/webhook', verifySignature, async (req, res) => { res.status(200).end(); enqueueForLLMProcessing(req.body) })`. Respond fast (200), then process async — webhook providers expect quick responses.

related_terms_explained.connection: Webhooks are inverted APIs — server calls client instead of client calling server.
Webhooks are common triggers for event-driven agents.

title: WebSockets (LLM)
short_definition: A bidirectional persistent-connection protocol used for real-time LLM applications like voice mode, live collaboration, and interruptible streaming.
full_explanation:
WebSockets establish a persistent, full-duplex TCP connection between client and server upgraded from HTTP. Unlike SSE (one-way streaming) or HTTP request/response (single-shot), WebSockets let both sides send messages anytime. For LLM applications, this enables: voice mode (audio chunks both directions), interruptible chat (user can cancel mid-generation), live collaboration (multiple users + LLM in shared context), and real-time tool execution feedback.

Key LLM applications using WebSockets:
- **OpenAI Realtime API**: WebSocket protocol for GPT-4o voice mode.
- **Voice agents**: bidirectional audio streaming for natural conversation.
- **Collaborative editing**: live code/document collab where the LLM is one of many participants.
- **Long-running tool execution**: client sends commands, LLM streams progress, intermediate results, and final output.

Tradeoffs vs SSE: WebSockets are more capable but more complex (state management, reconnection logic, library ceremony). For one-way token streaming, SSE is simpler and sufficient. For anything requiring client-to-server messages mid-generation (interruptions, audio input, command sequences), WebSockets are the right tool.

Production notes: behind certain firewalls, WebSocket connections fail where SSE works (corporate proxies sometimes block long-lived connections). Reconnection logic is essential — long-running connections drop occasionally and clients must rejoin gracefully.
why_it_matters: If you're building voice-first applications, real-time collaboration, or any LLM feature requiring bidirectional communication during generation, WebSockets are the right protocol. For text chat with one-way streaming, SSE is simpler. Knowing when each is appropriate is foundational network-layer literacy for LLM developers.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of SSE as a one-way radio (server broadcasts, client listens) and WebSockets as a phone line (both sides talk and listen). Voice mode needs a phone line — listening and speaking must overlap. Text chat with streaming output needs a radio — server broadcasts tokens, client listens. Pick the right tool for the conversation pattern.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI Realtime: `const ws = new WebSocket('wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime?model=gpt-4o-realtime-preview', {headers: {Authorization: 'Bearer ...'}}); ws.send(JSON.stringify({type:'session.update', ...}))`. Send audio chunks; receive audio + text events.

related_terms_explained.connection: OpenAI's Realtime API uses WebSockets for voice and text streaming.
WebSockets enable bidirectional streaming; SSE is the one-way alternative.
SSE is the simpler one-way alternative for LLM token streaming.
Voice mode interfaces typically rely on WebSockets for bidirectional audio.
